description: Build a simple web-based To-Do application using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Users can add new tasks, mark tasks as complete, and delete tasks from the list.

files.content: My To-Do /List
files.name: index.html
style.css
script.js

learningGoals: Structure a web page with HTML5 semantic elements.
Style a web application using CSS3 for a responsive and appealing design, including Flexbox.
Manipulate the DOM (Document Object Model) with JavaScript to add, update, and remove elements dynamically.
Implement event handling for user interactions (clicks, keyboard input).
Understand and apply event delegation for efficient event management on dynamic elements.

steps.explanation: Every web page starts with a fundamental HTML structure, often called boilerplate. This step covers the essential tags that define an HTML5 document, its character encoding, the page title, and how to link your external CSS and JavaScript files to make your app look good and be interactive. The `defer` attribute on the script tag is crucial here; it tells the browser to execute the script after the HTML is fully parsed, preventing potential errors from trying to manipulate elements that don't exist yet.
steps.hint: Don't forget the `<meta charset="UTF-8">` and `<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">` tags in the `<head>` for proper character handling and responsiveness. Use `<link rel="stylesheet" href="style.css">` and `<script src="script.js" defer></script>`.
steps.solution:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>My To-Do App</title>
    <!-- Link to our CSS file -->
    <link rel="stylesheet" href="style.css">
</head>
<body>
    <!-- Your app content will go here -->

    <!-- Link to our JavaScript file, 'defer' ensures HTML is parsed first -->
    <script src="script.js" defer></script>
</body>
</html>
steps.task: Create your `index.html` file. Add the basic HTML5 boilerplate, including `<!DOCTYPE html>`, `<html>`, `<head>`, and `<body>` tags. Set the page title to 'My To-Do App'. Link your CSS file (`style.css`) in the `<head>` and your JavaScript file (`script.js`) just before the closing `</body>` tag, making sure to include the `defer` attribute for the script.
steps.title: Basic HTML Document Setup

steps.explanation: To organize your application's content and simplify overall styling, it's common practice to wrap everything in a single main container `div`. This container will act as the central hub for your entire to-do app, making it easier to apply layout, background, and shadow styles to the entire interface later with CSS.
steps.hint: The `id` attribute should be unique within the HTML document. You'll target this `id` with CSS and JavaScript later.
steps.starterCode:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>My To-Do App</title>
    <link rel="stylesheet" href="style.css">
</head>
<body>
    <!-- Add your main app container here -->
    <script src="script.js" defer></script>
</body>
</html>
steps.task: Inside the `<body>` tag of your `index.html` file, create a `div` element. Give this `div` an `id` of `app`. This will serve as the main wrapper for all your to-do application's UI elements.
steps.title: Create the Main App Container

steps.explanation: Every application needs a clear title to let users know what it is. An `<h1>` tag is semantically important for the main heading of a page. It will display prominently at the top of our to-do application.
steps.hint: The `<h1>` tag should be the first element inside your `#app` container to logically represent the app's primary heading.
steps.solution: My To-Do List
steps.task: Inside the `<div id="app">` element, add an `<h1>` tag. Set its text content to 'My To-Do List'.
steps.title: Add the App Title

steps.explanation: This section is where users will interact with the app to add new tasks. It consists of an input field for typing the task description and a button to submit it. Grouping these related elements in a `div` will help with styling and layout later on.
steps.hint: Remember to close your tags correctly. The `placeholder` attribute provides a helpful hint to the user before they type.
steps.solution: My To-Do List
steps.starterCode: My To-Do List
steps.task:
Inside your `<div id="app">`, below the `<h1>`, create a new `div` with the class `input-area`. Inside this `input-area` div, add:
1. An `<input>` tag of `type="text"`, with an `id="task-input"` and a `placeholder="Add a new task..."`.
2. A `<button>` tag with an `id="add-task-btn"` and the text 'Add Task'.
steps.title: Build the Task Input Area

steps.explanation: This `<ul>` element will serve as the container where all your to-do tasks will be dynamically displayed. Initially, it will be empty, as JavaScript will be responsible for creating and inserting individual task items (`<li>` elements) into it as the user adds them.
steps.hint: The `<ul>` tag signifies an unordered list. It's empty for now, but JavaScript will fill it with tasks.
steps.solution: My To-Do List
steps.starterCode: My To-Do List
steps.task: Below the `input-area` div inside your `<div id="app">`, create an empty unordered list (`<ul>`) element. Give this `<ul>` an `id` of `task-list`.
steps.title: Prepare the Task List Container

steps.explanation: Before styling individual components, it's good practice to apply a CSS reset to ensure consistent rendering across different browsers. We'll also style the `body` to set a default font, background color, and use Flexbox to center our entire application horizontally and vertically on the page, creating a clean and focused layout.
steps.hint: The reset usually includes `margin: 0; padding: 0; box-sizing: border-box;`. For the body, choose a pleasant `font-family` and `background-color`.
steps.task: Create your `style.css` file. Add a basic CSS reset using the universal selector (`*`). Then, style the `body` element: set a `font-family`, `background-color`, and use `display: flex`, `justify-content: center`, `align-items: center`, `min-height: 100vh`, and `margin` to center the app.
steps.title: CSS Reset and Global Body Styling

steps.explanation: Now we'll make our `#app` container visually stand out from the page background. This involves setting its own background, adding some internal spacing (padding), rounding its corners, and giving it a subtle `box-shadow` to create depth, making it look like a card on the page.
steps.hint: Use a white or light `background-color` for the app. `max-width` is good for responsive design.
steps.task: Target the `#app` id in your `style.css`. Apply a `background-color`, `padding`, `border-radius`, `box-shadow`, `width`, and `max-width` to style the main container of your application.
steps.title: Style the Main App Container

steps.explanation: The `<h1>` element, which displays 'My To-Do List', should be clearly visible and centered to establish the app's title. We'll adjust its alignment, color, size, and add some bottom margin to separate it from the input area below.
steps.hint: A dark `color` works well for text against a light background. `margin-bottom` creates space below the heading.
steps.task: Style the `h1` element in your `style.css`. Set its `text-align` to `center`, choose a `color`, adjust `margin-bottom` for spacing, and increase `font-size` for prominence.
steps.title: Style the App Header

steps.explanation: This step focuses on making the task input field and the 'Add Task' button both functional and visually appealing. We'll use Flexbox to arrange them side-by-side, style their appearance, and add visual feedback for user interaction (focus for the input, hover for the button).
steps.hint: `flex-grow: 1` on the input makes it expand to fill available space. Use `transition` for smooth `:hover` effects.
steps.task: Style the `.input-area` div to `display: flex` to align its children. For `#task-input`, apply `flex-grow`, `padding`, `border`, `border-radius`, `font-size`, `outline`, and a `:focus` border color. For `#add-task-btn`, apply `padding`, `background-color`, `color`, `border`, `border-radius`, `cursor`, `margin-left`, and a `:hover` background color transition.
steps.title: Style the Task Input and Button

steps.explanation: This step styles the main task container (`#task-list`) and individual task items (`<li>` elements) which will be created by JavaScript. We'll remove default list styling, and use Flexbox on the `<li>` elements to neatly arrange the task text and action buttons (Complete/Delete) side-by-side, with proper spacing and visual appeal.
steps.hint: `list-style: none` is key for `ul`. Remember the `li` styles will apply to elements created dynamically. `word-break: break-word;` can prevent text overflow in `span`.
steps.task: Target `#task-list` and set `list-style: none` to remove bullet points. Then, define styles for `li` elements (which JS will create): `background-color`, `padding`, `margin-bottom`, `border-radius`, `box-shadow`. Use `display: flex`, `justify-content: space-between`, `align-items: center` for internal layout. Also, style the `span` within `li` to ensure task text properly fills space and wraps if long.
steps.title: Style the Task List and Items

steps.explanation: To visually distinguish completed tasks from active ones, we'll create a special CSS class, `.completed`. When JavaScript adds this class to a task's `<li>` element, these styles will automatically apply, providing immediate visual feedback to the user.
steps.hint: The selector `#task-list li.completed` targets `li` elements that are children of `#task-list` AND have the class `completed`.
steps.task: Create a CSS rule for `#task-list li.completed`. Apply `text-decoration: line-through` to strike through the text, change the `color` to a lighter shade (e.g., grey), and change the `background-color` to indicate it's completed.
steps.title: Style Completed Tasks

steps.explanation: The 'Complete' and 'Delete' buttons within each task need clear visual cues. We'll style them distinctly with different colors, padding, and subtle hover effects to make them easily identifiable and responsive to user interaction.
steps.hint: You can group common styles for both buttons (e.g., `.complete-btn, .delete-btn { ... }`) then define specific styles for each. Choose a blue for complete and red for delete.
steps.task: Define common styles for both `.complete-btn` and `.delete-btn`, such as `padding`, `border`, `border-radius`, `cursor`, `margin-left`. Then, apply specific `background-color` and `color` for each button type. Add distinct `:hover` effects (e.g., darker background color and a slight `transform: translateY(-1px)`).
steps.title: Style Task Action Buttons

steps.explanation: Before JavaScript can manipulate or react to elements on your HTML page, it needs to 'know' about them. This step involves getting references to the specific HTML elements (like the input field, add button, and the task list) that our script will interact with. We'll use `document.getElementById()` for this, as we've given our key elements unique IDs.
steps.hint: Ensure your IDs in JavaScript exactly match the IDs in your HTML. `const` is good for variables that won't be reassigned.
steps.task: Create your `script.js` file. Using `document.getElementById()`, get references to the HTML elements with IDs `task-input`, `add-task-btn`, and `task-list`. Store each reference in a `const` variable (e.g., `const taskInput = document.getElementById('task-input');`).
steps.title: Select DOM Elements

steps.explanation: This is the core logic for creating a new to-do item. The `addTask` function will take the text from the input field, validate it, then dynamically create all the necessary HTML elements (`<li>`, `<span>`, 'Complete' button, 'Delete' button) and assemble them into a new list item. Finally, it appends this new task to our `<ul>` and clears the input field for the next task.
steps.hint: Use `document.createElement('tagName')` to create new elements. `element.appendChild(child)` adds an element. `input.value.trim()` is useful for removing whitespace.
steps.task:
Define a function named `addTask`. Inside this function:
1. Get the trimmed value from `taskInput`. If it's empty, show an `alert` and `return`.
2. Create a new `<li>` element.
3. Create a `<span>` element, set its `textContent` to the task text.
4. Create a 'Complete' `<button>`, set its `textContent` to 'Complete', and add the class `complete-btn`.
5. Create a 'Delete' `<button>`, set its `textContent` to 'Delete', and add the class `delete-btn`.
6. Append the `<span>`, 'Complete' button, and 'Delete' button to the `<li>`.
7. Append the new `<li>` to `taskList`.
8. Clear `taskInput.value` and set focus back to `taskInput`.
steps.title: Implement `addTask` Function

steps.explanation: Now that we have the `addTask` function, we need a way to trigger it. This involves attaching event listeners to our 'Add Task' button and the input field. The button will trigger `addTask` on a `click` event, and the input field will trigger it when the 'Enter' key is pressed, providing a more intuitive user experience.
steps.hint: Use `element.addEventListener('event', functionName);`. For the `keydown` event, the event object passed to the listener contains information about the key pressed.
steps.task: Attach a `click` event listener to `addTaskBtn` that calls the `addTask` function. Also, attach a `keydown` event listener to `taskInput`. Inside this listener, check if `event.key` is `'Enter'`, and if so, call `addTask`.
steps.title: Add Event Listeners for New Tasks

steps.explanation:
In our to-do app, tasks are added dynamically by JavaScript. This means the 'Complete' and 'Delete' buttons don't exist in the HTML when the page first loads. If we tried to attach a separate event listener to *each* button as it's created, it would quickly become complicated and less efficient, especially with many tasks.

Instead, we'll use a powerful technique called **event delegation**. We'll attach just *one* `click` event listener to the `taskList` (the big `<ul>` container) which *is* always present on the page. When a user clicks *anywhere* inside the `taskList`, this single listener will catch the event.

Inside this listener, we can then check `event.target`. This special property tells us *exactly which element* was clicked.
- If the clicked element is a 'Complete' button, we'll find its parent `<li>` (the task item) and `toggle` a `completed` class on it. Toggling means if the class is there, it removes it; if it's not, it adds it. This will apply the strikethrough styling.
- If the clicked element is a 'Delete' button, we'll find its parent `<li>` and `remove()` it entirely from the page.

This approach is efficient because we only have one listener, and it works for all current and future tasks without needing to add new listeners every time a task is created.
steps.hint: Remember, `event.target` is your best friend here – it's the actual button that got clicked. To get to the task item (`<li>`), you'll need to access the `parentNode` of the button. The `classList.toggle()` method is perfect for switching the `completed` class on and off, which changes the task's visual style. And `element.remove()` will take care of deleting the task from the list entirely.
steps.task:
Now, let's make your tasks interactive! You'll add a single event listener to handle clicks on both 'Complete' and 'Delete' buttons within your task list.
1. Add a `click` event listener to your `taskList` (`<ul>` element).
2. Inside the listener function, get the `event.target` (the element that was clicked) and store it in a `const target` variable.
3. Use an `if` statement to check if `target` has the class `complete-btn` (using `target.classList.contains('complete-btn')`). If it does, toggle the `completed` class on the `target.parentNode` (the `<li>` task item).
4. Add another `if` statement to check if `target` has the class `delete-btn`. If it does, remove `target.parentNode` from the DOM using `remove()`.
steps.title: Implement Event Delegation for Task Actions

title: Simple To-Do App
